feedback.text: A small thing first, about influencers, I implore you to consider carefully any new regulations, as the majority of online creators are not rich, and a good amount of them rely on sponsorships for their livelihood. I've seen deceptive marketing coming from them, I am not questioning that. I am saying only that you very much ought to keep in mind that they are workers too. Entertainment workers, but workers still. Now I would like to add to those findings of the Digital Fairness Fitness Check. Tech companies have a tendency to begin selling software for some time, to then take some unexpected turn the consumer could not have accounted for when paying, like adopting a subscription model (Adobe did that), or, completely discontinuing it and in the end letting the consumer both having spent money, and having kept no software. I am concerned with the latter especially because it's common in the gaming space, which is a special case because it's also a matter of erasing art. And because I would rather not have some of my fondest memories be erased. Old digital "footprints" are my only mementos of some people. That's why I want sold software to have a clear, knowable to the consumer before any payments, end of life plan that ideally guarantees a functional state for the software after support for it ends.
